coredata.dc:title: Amphiphilic carbon dots as high-efficiency corrosion inhibitor for N80 steel in HCl solution: Performance and mechanism investigation
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Yin
Lu, Renling
Chen, Wu
Mei, Ping
Lai, Lu
coredata.dc:description: Three amphiphilic carbon dots (CDs, C6-CDs, C8-CDs, and C12-CDs) were synthesized and first adopted as a corrosion inhibitor for mild steel. The inhibition behavior of amphiphilic CDs for N80 steel in hydrochloric acid solution was studied via weight loss tests, surface characterization, and electrochemical measurements. Results confirmed that they could protect N80 steel effectively; the longer the carbon chain was, the better the inhibition effect was. Specifically, the inhibition efficiency of C12-CDs reached 92.9 % at 150 mg/L. Moreover, due to the fluorescence characteristics of CDs, the changes in adsorption film during corrosion could be directly observed under UV light. A dense adsorption film was formed through the electrostatic interaction with CDs and the metal surface. Long carbon chains formed a hydrophobic layer on the surface of N80 to isolate the corrosive medium.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Amphiphilic carbon dots
Corrosion inhibitor
Fluorescence detection
Adsorptive film

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structural and morphology analysis of amphiphilic CDs
Effect of immersion time on corrosion inhibition performance of CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The synthetic routes of three amphiphilic CDs.
FTIR, XRD and UV–vis of as-prepared CDs (a) FTIR; (b) XRD; (c) UV–vis.
XPS spectra of as-prepared CDs (a) XPS; (b) XPS-C1s C6-CDs; (c) XPS-C1s C8-CDs; (d) XPS-C1s C12-CDs.
TEM, particle size distribution diagrams (inset) and HRTEM images of (a) C6-CDs; (b) C8-CDs; (c) C12-CDs.
Photos of the as-prepared CDs in different solvents in UV light (upper) and daylight (bottom).
EIS images of N80 steel in various test solutions (a1) (a2) (a3) C6-CDs; (b1) (b2) (b3) C8-CDs; (c1) (c2) (c3) C12-CDs.
Tafel curves of N80 steel in various test solutions (a) C6-CDs; (b) C8-CDs; (c) C12-CDs.
Variation of C R and ƞ % ((a) C6-CDs; (b) C8-CDs; (c) C12-CDs) with immersion time. The concentrations of CDs is 150 mg/L.
Photos of the N80 steel under daylight (left) and UV light (right) after prefilming (a) and corrosion (b).
Corrosion morphology of N80 steel in various solutions (a) blank; (b) C6-CDs; (c) C8-CDs; (d) C12-CDs.
XPS spectra of steel surface after corrosion test in blank and CDs solutions.
Langmuir adsorption isotherms of N80 steel surface in various test solutions (a) C6-CDs; (b) C8-CDs; (c) C12-CDs.
Protective mechanisms of CDs to N80 steel in 1 M HCl solution.
The relative contents of C, N, and O atoms for the three amphiphilic CDs.
Impedance parameters of N80 steel in various test solutions.
Corrosion parameters of N80 steel in various test solutions.
The corrosion rate and percentage inhibition at different immersion times.

head.ce:title: Amphiphilic carbon dots as high-efficiency corrosion inhibitor for N80 steel in HCl solution: Performance and mechanism investigation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Yangtze University, Jingzhou 434023, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Yangtze University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Yangtze University, Jingzhou 434023, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Three amphiphilic carbon dots (CDs, C6-CDs, C8-CDs, and C12-CDs) were synthesized and first adopted as a corrosion inhibitor for mild steel. The inhibition behavior of amphiphilic CDs for N80 steel in hydrochloric acid solution was studied via weight loss tests, surface characterization, and electrochemical measurements. Results confirmed that they could protect N80 steel effectively; the longer the carbon chain was, the better the inhibition effect was. Specifically, the inhibition efficiency of C12-CDs reached 92.9 % at 150 mg/L. Moreover, due to the fluorescence characteristics of CDs, the changes in adsorption film during corrosion could be directly observed under UV light. A dense adsorption film was formed through the electrostatic interaction with CDs and the metal surface. Long carbon chains formed a hydrophobic layer on the surface of N80 to isolate the corrosive medium.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon steel is widely used in pipelines and other fields because of its low cost and high flexibility [1,2]. Hydrochloric acid (HCl) and sulfuric acid are often used to clean the scale in equipment and pipelines in industrial production, but these strong acids often cause the corrosion of metal materials [3,4]. Adding corrosion inhibitors is an effective method to reduce metal corrosion.5 However, in recent years, with the growing pursuit of environmental protection, traditional corrosion inhibitors cannot meet the needs of the public because they will harm the environment when discharged.

Carbon dots (CDs) are new fluorescent carbon nanomaterials in the size range of 1–10 nm [5]. Given their unique fluorescence property and biocompatibility, CDs are often used in the biological field [6–8]. In recent years, CDs have also been found to play a specific role in corrosion protection. Cui et al. [9] first reported the corrosion inhibition performance of CDs in 2017. They synthesized CDs from 4-aminosalicylic acid and found that the inhibition efficiency of the CDs was 87.2 % at 100 mg/L in 1 M HCl. Subsequently, p-CDs and o-CDs were synthesized by Cui et al. [10] from p-phenylenediamine and o-phenylenediamine, respectively. Their inhibition efficiency accounted for 88 % and 94 % at 200 mg/L in 1 M HCl. In another report, JieLv et al. [11] selected citric acid and diethylenetriamine as raw materials to prepare N-doped CDs. The as-prepared CDs reached the best inhibition performance (81.2 %) at 600 mg/L in 1 M HCl.

For conventional organic corrosion inhibitors, the corresponding studies have shown that the hydrophobic chain can form a hydrophobic layer between the adsorption film and corrosion medium to hinder the invasion of the corrosion medium [12]. Thus, the hydrophobic groups significantly affect the inhibition performance [13]. However, the current research on CDs, as a corrosion inhibitor, is often only hydrophilic. How about the corrosion inhibition performance of amphiphilic CDs? Therefore, we synthesized three amphiphilic CDs with different carbon chain lengths from citric acid and alkyl amine (hexylamine, n-octylamine, and dodecylamine) by using a simple one-step pyrolysis method. The surface of these CDs had both hydrophilic and hydrophobic groups. Thus, these CDs had good solubility and fluorescence properties in water and many organic solvents with different polarities. We found that these three CDs had excellent corrosion inhibition performance, and the fluorescence properties of the CDs could be used to observe the integrality of adsorptive films.

Citric acid monohydrate (AR, 99.5 %, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.), hexylamine (99 %, Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd.), n-octylamine (99 %, Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd.), dodecylamine (98 %, Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd.), and HCl (36–38 %, Macklin Reagent Co., Ltd.) were used as received. N80 specimens with dimensions of 40 mm × 13 mm× 2 mm were used in weight loss tests and prefilming treatments. The contact area of each N80 specimen was ~12.52 cm2. The exposed surface of the working electrode was 1.0 cm2. Before usage, the working electrode was ultrasonically cleaned with acetone and absolute ethanol and ground with various grades of emery paper up to 4500 grit.

Three amphiphilic CDs were synthesized through a simple synthetic route, as shown in Fig. 1. Citric acid (2.0 g) and hexylamine (2.0 g) were added to 50 mL three-neck flasks and heated at 180 ℃ for 3 h in oil baths. The obtained reaction products were then dissolved in absolute ethanol. Next, the amphiphilic CD solution was centrifuged at 10,000 r/min for 15 min to remove the dissolved material. After the removal of the solvent under low pressure, the products were dissolved in ultrapure water and dialyzed (1 kDa dialysis membrane) for 12 h. Finally, C6-CDs were obtained by freeze-drying. Hexamine was replaced with n-octylamine and dodecylamine, and C8-CDs and C12-CDs were prepared in the same way. The three prepared amphiphilic CDs had good solubility in water and other organic solvents with different polarities.

The infrared spectra of the amphiphilic CDs were obtained using a 6700 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Thermo Nicolet, USA). The elemental composition and corresponding chemical valence states of the amphiphilic CDs were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, PHI 5000 VersaProbe III, Japan). A powder X-ray diffractometer (PANalytical Empyrean Co., Ltd., UK) was used to determine the XRD diffraction patterns of the amphiphilic CDs. Field-emission transmission electron microscopy analysis was carried out using JEM-2100 F (JEOL, Japan) operating at 200 kV with a superthin carbon coat. The UV–vis absorption spectrum was measured on a TU1810 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Beijing Purkinje General Instrument, China).

A CHI-660E electrochemical workstation performed electrochemical tests at 298 K. The corrosion medium was 1 M HCl solution containing different concentrations of amphiphilic CDs. A three-electrode system was used, with the N80 specimen as the working electrode, the platinum plate electrode as the counter electrode, and the saturated calomel electrode as the reference electrode. The measurement started after the open-circuit potential (OCP) reached the steady state (the potential fluctuation was less than 250 mV). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was tested at 105–10−2 Hz with an amplitude of 5 mV. The potential range of potentiodynamic polarization (Tafel) curves was the OCP plus or minus 250 mV, and the scanning speed was 1 mV/s.

In accordance with ASTM G16–13 (2019), the corrosion inhibition efficiency (ƞ, %) and rate of corrosion (C R) were evaluated by weight loss analysis. For the reproducibility of the experiment, each test was repeated 3 times. The N80 specimens were immersed in 500 mL of 1 M HCl solution with 150 mg/L of inhibitors, and the weight loss at different times was measured.

The corresponding experiments were designed based on the prefilming treatment of pipelines and the fluorescence properties of C12-CDs [14]. First, to better observe the adsorption film of C12-CDs, only half of each steel sheet was immersed in a C12-CDs solution (solvent: acetone; 500 mg/L) for two hours, and the remaining half was used as a control. After that, the sheet was washed with distilled water, acetone, and absolute ethanol successively and then dried in an N2 atmosphere. Finally, the whole sheet was immersed in HCl solution (1 mol/L) for 12 h to observe the integrity of the C12-CDs adsorption film during the corrosion process. A fluorescence and ultraviolet spectrophotometer can also be used to quantitatively analyze the residual concentration of C12-CDs.

After the weight loss tests, the N80 specimen was washed with distilled water and dried in a N2 atmosphere. The surface morphology of the N80 samples was observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Zeiss Sigma 300, Germany). In addition, the N80 samples were tested by XPS (PHI 5000 VersaProbe III, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morphology analysis of amphiphilic CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 2, among the three amphiphilic CDs prepared, only C6-CDs could observe the absorption bands related to N-H stretching vibration at 3305 cm−1. It might be due to the longer carbon chains of C8-CDs and C12-CDs, which made the N-H stretching vibration absorption less evident [15]. The two strong peaks at 2926 and 2856 cm−1 were related to the stretching vibration of the C-H bond [16], and the difference in these peak intensities of the three CDs could be seen. Amide bonding from the stretching vibration of the CO bond and the bending peak of the N-H bond appeared at 1696 and 1645 cm−1, respectively. The characteristic stretch of the amide Ⅲ C-N bond was at 1542 cm−1 [17]. These amide functional groups indicated that CDs had been successfully prepared [18].

XPS analyzed the bonding state of the elements of CDs. The XPS full spectra of C6-CDs, C8-CDs, and C12-CDs and the Gaussian fitting results of C1s are shown in Fig. 3(a)-(d). Comparison of the full XPS spectra of the three amphiphilic CDs revealed three apparent peaks at 288, 402, and 529 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively, indicating that the three CDs were all composed of C, N, and O elements. The proportion of each element measured is shown in Table 1. From the data in the table, with the increase in carbon chain length, the N element of CDs decreased, whereas the C element increased. The XPS high-resolution diagram of each CDs in Fig. 3(b)-(d) shows that there were three fitting peaks in the C1s fitting spectrum, which were located at 284.78, 285.98, and 288.33 eV, corresponding to the valence states of the three kinds of carbon: sp2C(CC/C-C), sp3C(C-N), and sp2C(CO), respectively [19]. In the comparison of the intensity of the fitting peaks, a clear pattern could be seen: the intensity of the conjugate structure peaks with sp2 hybridization from C6-CDs to C12-CDs showed an increasing trend, indicating that the graphite-like conjugate structure in CDs was gradually increasing.

To determine the internal carbonization structure state of CDs, C6-CDs, C8-CDs, and C12-CDs were characterized by XRD. The results are shown in Fig. 2. The XRD curves of all three amphiphilic CDs showed a strong diffraction peak at around 2θ = 20°. The position of this diffraction peak corresponded to the crystallographic diffraction peak of the 002 surfaces in the graphite-like material structure, further confirming the existence of a graphite-like structure in the core of the CDs [20]. The longer the carbon chain was, the higher the degree of graphitization was, consistent with the XPS results.

TEM was used to characterize the microstructural morphology of the three amphiphilic CDs. The TEM images of C6-CDs, C8-CDs, and C12-CDs are shown in Fig. 4. The three CDs were in the form of nanoparticles, and all of them had good dispersion without pronounced agglomeration. The particle size distributions of the CDs were statistically calculated in accordance with the DigitalMicrograph software, and the results showed that the average sizes of C6-CDs, C8-CDs, and C12-CDs were 3.2, 4.2, and 4.5 nm, respectively. The sizes were relatively concentrated in the particle size distribution range. Although the average size of CDs had increased, it was still in the particle size range of 3–5 nm. The HRTEM images shown in Fig. 4(a2)-(c2) suggested that the three amphiphilic CDs had visible crystalline lattice fringes. The interlayer space was about 0.19 nm, corresponding to the d-spacing of the graphene (1−100) planes [21]. These results were in good agreement with the XRD profiles.

The UV–vis absorption of the three amphiphilic CDs had characteristic peaks at 238 and 335 nm. In Fig. 2, 238 nm was attributed to the π–π* electronic transition of conjugate structure, and 335 nm was the n–π* electronic transition of CN bond [22]. In Fig. 5, the daylight and UV photographs of the three amphiphilic CDs dissolved in water and different polar solvents are displayed. All three CDs had strong fluorescence in water and organic solvents, confirming their amphiphilic properties. In water, C6-CDs had the most vigorous fluorescence intensity. In cyclohexane, C12-CDs had the strongest fluorescence intensity. Hence, the hydrophilicity of the CDs could be effectively regulated by changing the carbon chain length of the raw material.

The Nyquist and Bode diagrams of N80 in 1 M HCl solution with different concentrations of amphiphilic CDs at 298 K are shown in Fig. 6. From the Nyquist plots, the addition of CDs efficaciously extended the diameter of the impedance arc, in which C12-CDs with the longest carbon chain had the largest impedance arc diameter. In addition, the density of the protecting film improved [23], and the capacitive reactance value increased with the increase in the concentration of the three CDs. The Bode plots in Fig. 6 stood for the two curves of impedance modulus and phase angle for the frequency range of 105–10−2. The high-frequency region, when the current was all conducted by the capacitor, presented a horizontal line [24]; between the high frequency and low frequency was a line with a negative slope; and the low-frequency region, when the resistor conducted the current, exhibited a horizontal line [25]. From the Bode plots, the system had only a time constant, which indicated that the corrosion of N80 carbon steel in HCl solution was mainly controlled by the charge transfer process [26]. The addition of CDs significantly increased R ct, which meant that CDs could effectively inhibit the charge transfer process of corrosion. Under the medium-frequency condition, the peak increased gradually with the increase in CDs concentration, and the peak of C12-CDs was also the largest. The C dl value showed a reverse trend to the R ct value. This observation was due to that the corrosion medium on the steel surface was gradually superseded by CDs [27,28].

The experimental data were fitted and analyzed on the basis of the impedance spectra and system characteristics by using the equivalent circuit diagram shown in Fig. 7. In this equivalent circuit diagram, R s is the solution resistance between the study electrode surface and the reference electrode; R ct represents the charge transfer resistance of the corrosion reaction; and CPE is the constant phase angle element, which is often used to replace the bilayer capacitance (C dl) on the metal electrode surface to more accurately resolve the bilayer impedance effect on the working electrode surface. Corrosion parameters, such as R s, R ct, and C dl, are listed in Table 2, where C dl is calculated in accordance with the following equation [29–31]:
(1)
C
d l
=
(
Y
0
R
c t
1 − n
)
1 n
where Y 0 is the exponential factor; R ct is the charge transfer resistance; and n is the dispersion coefficient, and its magnitude is related to the metal surface morphology. According to the Helmholtz model, C dl can also be expressed as follows [8]:
(2)
C
d l
=
ε ° ε
d
S
where ε
° is the air dielectric constant, ε is the dielectric constant, S is the surface area of the working electrode, and d is the thickness of the adsorbed film. From this equation, the decrease in dielectric constant, the increase in adsorption area, and the increase in film thickness all decrease the value of double-layer capacitance. The inhibition efficiency (η) can be obtained through the following equation [28]:
(3)
η
E I S
=
R
c t
−
R
c t , 0
R
c t
× 100 %
where R ct and R ct,0 are the charge transfer resistance of metal corrosion in the added corrosion inhibitor and blank solution, respectively. As the concentration of CDs increased, the value of η increased accordingly. At the same concentration, the η of C12-CDs was higher than those of C8-CDs and C6-CDs. When the concentration of C12-CDs was 150 mg/L, the η value reached 92.6 %. The results showed that the longer the carbon chain was, the better the protective effect of CDs on steel in HCl solution was.

Fig. 8 shows the Tafel curves of N80 steel in 1 M HCl solution with different concentrations of amphiphilic CDs. The electrochemical parameters, such as corrosion potential (E corr), corrosion density (i corr), and Tafel slope of anode and cathode (β a, β c) fitted by extrapolation, are shown in Table 3. The corrosion inhibition efficiency (η) can be calculated as follows [32]:
(4)
η
T a f e l
=
i
c o r r , 0
−
i
c o r r
i
c o r r , 0
× 100 %
where i corr,0, and i corr represent the current densities of 1 M HCl without and with inhibitors, respectively. Fig. 8 depicts that as the concentration of amphiphilic CDs increased, the cathodic and anodic polarization curves shifted in the direction of decreasing current density. From the data in Table 3, the corrosion potential of 150 mg/L of C6-CDs, C8-CDs, and C12-CDs was lower than that of the blank solution by 24, 25, and 27 mV, respectively, which were all less than 85 mV. C12-CDs decreased the most. This result proved that all three CDs belong to mixed corrosion inhibitors [33]. Owing to the similar changing trends of β a and β c of the three corrosion inhibitors, the inhibition mechanism of CDs prepared by different carbon chains had not changed. Compared with the blank solution, the β
a in the CD solution had a significant change, resulting from the adsorption of corrosion inhibitors on the active anode [34]. Therefore, the three CDs were all a kind of mixed corrosion inhibitor that mainly inhibited the anodic reaction [35]. After the addition of the three CDs, i corr was significantly reduced. As the concentration of the amphiphilic CDs increased, i corr showed a downward trend, implying that the corrosion rate was reduced. Simultaneously, ƞ could also reflect its inhibitory ability. As the concentration of CDs increased, the inhibitory ability was significantly enhanced. When the dosage of C6-CDs, C8-CDs, and C12-CDs was 150 mg/L, the corrosion inhibition rates were 87.9 %, 88.4 %, and 92.1 %, respectively. As the carbon chain grew, the inhibition efficiency of CDs became significantly larger, indicating that the carbon chain could be effective in suppressing the corrosion of N80 steel. The previous results agreed with the EIS findings, which indicated that amphiphilic CDs rich in carbon chains could provide an ideal inhibitory effect. The increase in slow-release performance might be associated with the sorption of the protective coating on the carbon steel surface [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of immersion time on corrosion inhibition performance of CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The weight loss method is one of the simple and reliable methods to test the performance of corrosion inhibitors. N80 specimens were immersed in HCl solution (1 M) and inhibitors (150 mg/L). The changing trend of inhibition efficiency of amphiphilic CDs with time was detected through immersion for different times (1, 8, 16, and 24 h). The weight loss data shown in Fig. 9 and Table 4 include the corrosion inhibition efficiency (ƞ %) and corrosion rate (C R) of C6-CDs, C8-CDs, and C12-CDs at different immersion times. C R and ƞ % were evaluated using the following equations [26]:
(5)
C
R
=
87.6 w
a t D
(6)
η =
C
R
0
−
C
R
i
C
R
0
× 100 %
where w is the corrosion weight loss of N80 (mg), a is the area of the coupon (cm2), t is the exposure time (h), and D is the density of N80 (7.85 g·cm−3). Fig. 9 shows that the corrosion rate increased with exposure time. From Table 4, after 1 h immersion, the corrosion inhibition rates of C6-CDs, C8-CDs, and C12-CDs were 97.3 %, 97.2 %, and 98.8 %, respectively; after 24 h immersion, the corrosion inhibition rates were 85.7 %, 88.0 %, and 92.9 %, respectively. According to Fig. 9, with the increase in immersion time, the corrosion rates gradually increased, while the corrosion inhibition efficiencies gradually decreased. The dissolution of the metal surface caused the protective layer to become uneven and unstable to promote the desorption of corrosion inhibitors [37,38]. However, the trend of corrosion rate growth and the decrease in inhibition efficiency after 24 h was gradually smoothed out because the desorption rate would remain stable when the dissolution and adsorption rates reached equilibrium [39]. This finding confirmed that CDs had a lasting corrosion inhibition effect.

Before being used, the steel pipelines were pre-passivating treatment with a high concentration corrosion inhibitor solution, which generates a dense protective film on the metal surface. After that, only a low concentration corrosion inhibitor solution was needed to ensure the integrity of the protective film during the subsequent production process. Due to the fluorescence properties of CDs, the performance of the pre-passivating treatment or the integrity of the adsorption film of CDs could be detected directedly by ultraviolet light. As shown in Fig. 10 a2, a complete adsorption film of CDs can be observed under ultraviolet light after immersing N80 specimens in CDs solution. Moreover, after the following immersing in HCl (1 mol/L) for 12 h, the upper part of N80 specimens without pre-passivating treatment was corroded severely, while no severe corrosion occurred in the lower part of N80 specimens, as shown in Fig. 10 b1. Although the fluorescence intensity of the N80 surface decreased obviously after immersing in HCl solution, the integrity of the adsorption film can be observed directly. In addition, the loss of fluorescence might be related to the dissolution of the CDs adsorptive film in acidic media or the effects of acid on the fluorescence intensity of CDs [40]. The experimental results showed that the change in the fluorescence intensity of CDs adsorption film could be observed after pre-passivating treatment.

For practical applications, it is essential to accurately determine the residual concentration of corrosion inhibitors, which impacts the running costs. Fig. S1 showed the relationship between the concentration of C12-CDs and fluorescence intensity at 435 nm and UV absorbance at 238 nm in 1 mg/L–100 mg/L. It could be seen from Fig. S1 that the fluorescence intensity and UV absorbance had a good positive linear correlation with the concentration of C12-CDs. Therefore, the residual concentration of CDs solution could be analyzed by fluorescence spectrometry and UV spectrophotometry.

The N80 steel specimens were soaked in 1 M HCl solution without and with amphiphilic CDs after fine grinding with sandpaper step by step, and SEM tests were conducted after soaking. The corrosion morphology is shown in Fig. 11. The surface of N80 steel after immersing in 1 M HCl solution is depicted in Fig. 11(a), which was corroded seriously. However, Fig. 11b-d, after CDs were added, the surface of the N80 sample was relatively smooth, the surface of the steel adsorbed by C12-CDs was the smoothest, and the degree of corrosion was the lightest.

Meanwhile, the corresponding XPS ( Fig. 12) showed that the content of Fe on the N80 surface was significantly reduced after the addition of CDs, indicating that the CDs effectively covered the N80 surface, and the metal surface covered by C12-CDs had the lowest Fe content. In the presence of the three CDs, the oxygen content decreased. The oxidation reaction on the steel surface was hindered by the adsorption of CD molecules, so the oxygen content of the N80 steel surface decreased after the addition of CDs. The appearance of elemental N on the surface of N80 steel immersed in the CD solution further indicated that CDs formed coordination bonds with metallic iron on the surface of N80 steel, producing an adsorption film. C6-CDs had the most N element content, which might be due to the short carbon chain and the high N content of C6-CDs itself. The surface analysis results were consistent with the electrochemical test results. The above results indicated that the corrosion inhibition of CDs was due to the formation of adsorption film on the surface of carbon steel; thus, the activity of metal reactions was reduced, and the corrosion inhibition was achieved. The longer the carbon chain was, the more robust the adsorptive capacity on the metal surface was.

The adsorption isotherm can be obtained by [41]:
(7)
C θ
=
1
K
a d s
+ C
where C is the concentration of CDs, θ is the surface coverage, and K ads is the adsorption isotherm. On the basis of the surface coverage θ calculated from the weightlessness test, the linear fit was plotted by C/θ against C, as shown in Fig. 13. The correlation coefficients R 2 of 0.99994, 0.99993, and 0.99999 for C6-CDs, C8-CDs, and C12-CDs, respectively, indicated that all three fitted curves had a good correlation. The results showed that the adsorption process of three CDs followed the Langmuir adsorption. After calculation, the K ads values of C6-CDs, C8-CDs, and C12-CDs were 886.52, 1358.70, and 2150.54, respectively. Generally, the adsorption effect became better with K ads value. That is, the inhibitory effect of CDs could be evaluated by K ads [42]. Among the three kinds of amphiphilic CDs, C12-CDs had the highest K ads, indicating that it had the strongest adsorption capacity and the best protection effect on the steel surface in contact with the solution. The following equation can calculate the Gibbs free energy (
Δ
G
a d s
0
) [43]:
(8)
Δ
G
a d s
0
= − R T
ln ( 1000
K
a d s
)
where R is the molecular gas constant (8.314 J·mol−1·K−1), and T corresponds to the adsorption temperature (298 K). Generally, when
Δ
G
a d s
0
is higher than − 20 kJ/mol, the adsorption of corrosion inhibitors on the metal surface is a physical process. The charge of corrosion inhibitors is opposite to that on the metal surface, and they produce physical adsorption due to electrostatic attraction. When
Δ
G
a d s
0
is − 40 kJ/mol or more negative, covalent bonding or coordination bonding occurs between the inhibitor molecule and the metal surface, which is a chemical adsorption process. However, when the value of
Δ
G
a d s
0
is between − 20 and − 40 kJ/mol, physical adsorption and chemical adsorption exist simultaneously [44]. After calculation, the
Δ
G
a d s
0
values of C6-CDs, C8-CDs, and C12-CDs were about − 33.93, − 34.99, and − 36.12 kJ/mol, respectively, indicating that chemical adsorption and physical adsorption were formed on the steel surface in contact with the solution at the same time. Merging the former XPS spectra and FTIR confirmed that the CDs contained electron-rich N, O, and electron-rich conjugated constitutions. Hence, they could be regarded as the CDs containing electron-rich functional groups coordinating with metals. The chelation formed complexes and created a thick protective film to separate the reaction between the outside environment and the metal surface to prevent metal corrosion.

The adsorption mechanism of the prepared amphiphilic CDs in 1 M HCl is shown in Fig. 14. According to adsorption isotherm profiling, the three CDs existed on the surface of N80 carbon steel simultaneously in the form of physical adsorption and chemical adsorption. The chemical adsorption was attributed to the N atoms on the CDs. Owing to lone pairs of electrons, the unoccupied three-dimensional orbitals outside the iron atom were easily filled and subsequently formed coordination bonds between the heteroatoms and the steel. In addition, the electrostatic interaction between CDs and free metal ions led them to gather on the metal surface in the form of physical adsorption. Long-carbon-chain amphiphilic CDs could be aligned on the metal surface to form a dense protective layer due to their hydrophobic chains [45]; as a result of their wealth of hydrophilic polar groups, they could better interact with metals. Given its long carbon chain, C12-CDs could be evenly adsorbed on the surface of N80 steel to form a high-strength and thick adsorption film to reduce the exposed area of steel in corrosive mediums, and the corrosion inhibition rate was the highest. By contrast, due to the shorter carbon chain of C6-CDs and C8-CDs, the thinner adsorption film had lower strength, and the steel surface was easily corroded by the corrosive solution, forming large-area corrosion pits. In short, the corrosion inhibition mechanism of the prepared amphiphilic CDs could be regarded as that CDs with the structure of nitrogen oxide graphene produce an electrostatic interaction with the metal surface. At the same time, the amphiphilic CDs were oriented on the metal surface through the long carbon chains. The chemical bond enhanced the adsorption force and formed a dense protective layer. The longer the carbon chain was, the thicker the protective layer was to improve the corrosion inhibition performance.

In this study, three amphiphilic CDs with a long alkyl chain were successfully prepared and applied in the corrosion inhibition of N80 in acid for the first time. In accordance with the experimental and theoretical research, the following conclusions were obtained:
(1) The results of FTIR and XPS showed that the length of the carbon chain of raw material could effectively adjust the proportion of hydrophilic and hydrophobic groups in amphiphilic CDs.

(2) Tafel and EIS results showed that C6-CDs, C8-CDs, and C12-CDs could effectively protect N80 in 1 M HCl; the longer the carbon chain was, the better the corrosion inhibition effect was. According to the weight loss method, at 150 mg/L, the corrosion inhibition efficiencies of C6-CDs, C8-CDs, and C12-CDs were 85.7 %, 88.0 %, and 92.9 %, respectively, after 24 h immersion.

(3) The observation of surface morphology demonstrated that the corrosion degree of N80 steel decreased significantly after CDs were added, and the amphiphilic CDs could form a stable adsorption protective film on the surface of N80 steel.
(4) The adsorption process of the three amphiphilic CDs on the N80 steel were all chemical adsorption and physical adsorption, which conformed to the Langmuir adsorption isotherm.
Yin Yang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Renling Lu: Data curation. Wu Chen: Visualization, Investigation. Ping Mei: Supervision. Lu Lai: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes (Tiangong University, No. M202109) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant no. 22073025, 21873075).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2022.129457.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
